items.q: What is the behaviourist view on the nature-nurture debate?
items.correct: Strongly nurture - we are shaped by environmental conditioning.
items.why: Behaviourists reject innate ideas (tabula rasa - blank slate). Students soften this to 'a mix'. Watson and Skinner explicitly attributed nearly all behaviour to environment.

items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: Top-down offender profiling was developed by:
items.why: It's FBI. “Canter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Differential association theory was proposed by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sutherland. “Eysenck” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Lombroso publish his atavistic form theory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1876 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
